formulas.name: Aristotle's Concept of Catharsis
formulas.formula: Catharsis is the emotional purging of the audience, relieving them of their pent-up emotions.
formulas.notes: Aristotle's concept of catharsis is a key element of tragedy, allowing the audience to experience emotional release.

formulas.name: Shakespeare's Dramatic Irony
formulas.formula: Dramatic irony occurs when the audience knows something that the characters do not.
formulas.notes: Shakespeare's use of dramatic irony creates a sense of tension and suspense in his plays.

formulas.name: Tone and Mood in Literature
formulas.formula: Tone refers to the author's attitude towards the subject, while mood is the atmosphere or feeling evoked in the reader.
formulas.notes: Understanding tone and mood is crucial in analyzing and interpreting literary texts.

formulas.name: Symbolism in Literature
formulas.formula: A symbol is an object, color, or image that represents an abstract idea or concept.
formulas.notes: Symbols are used to convey deeper meanings and themes in literature.

formulas.name: Foreshadowing in Literature
formulas.formula: Foreshadowing is a literary device that hints at events that will occur later in the text.
formulas.notes: Foreshadowing creates suspense and anticipation in the reader.

formulas.name: Irony in Literature
formulas.formula: Irony occurs when there is a discrepancy between what is expected and what actually happens.
formulas.notes: Irony is used to highlight the absurdity or contradiction in a situation.

formulas.name: Characterization in Literature
formulas.formula: Characterization is the process of creating and developing characters in a literary text.
formulas.notes: Effective characterization is essential in making characters relatable and believable.
